output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.7, "R1.2": 0.95, "R1.3": 0.8, "R1.4": 0.9, "R1.5": 0.4, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.85}}

Summary: Flagged a load-bearing contradiction with the cited prior work, tightened claims to match the evidence actually reported, and produced a rewritten draft.

## Key issues to fix before this is publishable

**1. The novelty claim contradicts the cited baseline.** Paragraph 1 says LenSlice is "the first end-to-end system to make length debiasing a property of the serving layer itself." Paragraph 3 then says Reyes and Tanaka (2023)'s JudgeStream "pioneered inline residualization for LLM judges." Both statements cannot stand. Either (a) JudgeStream is the first and LenSlice is a refinement (faster, different routing, different residualization), or (b) JudgeStream is something narrower than the current text concedes (e.g., not deployed as a serving system, or applies a different correction). The contribution paragraph must state the actual delta against JudgeStream — likely some combination of routing granularity (per-decile shards vs. monolithic), the throughput gain, or that JudgeStream did offline-fitted residualization while LenSlice keeps reference pools live. Right now, the throughput line (1,840 vs 1,200/sec) is the only concrete delta, and that is a systems result, not a debiasing-methodology novelty.

**2. Verify the Reyes & Tanaka (2023) / "JudgeStream" citation.** I do not recognize this work; if it is fabricated or misattributed the whole framing collapses. Please confirm against your reference manager before submission.

**3. "Decile" is the wrong word.** Deciles are population-based 10-quantile bins. The brackets you list (50–100, 100–200, …, 1600+) are fixed token-range bins and there appear to be more than ten. Use "length bin" or "length bucket." Relatedly, "hashed into a length decile" should be "bucketed by length" — there is no hash function here.

**4. Specify the residual length-bias coefficient.** "Reduces the residual length-bias coefficient from 0.43 to 0.04" is meaningless without saying what regression this is the coefficient of. Standard practice: report β from a logistic regression of judge-preference on (log) length-delta, with units. Also report a confidence interval — a point estimate alone is not credible at this scale.

**5. The evaluation is thinner than the framing implies.** One benchmark (AlpacaEval-2), one judge (GPT-4-Turbo). The abstract-level claim that debiasing is now a "first-class property of the serving path" needs at least a second judge family (e.g., Claude or an open-weights judge) to rule out that you have over-fit to GPT-4-Turbo's particular length sensitivity. If a second judge is out of scope for this paper, soften the claim accordingly.

**6. Underspecified mechanics.** Three things a reader will trip on:
- *Reference pool composition.* "Length-matched reference completions" — are these gold outputs, prior production completions, or judge-tied anchors? Critical to the method.
- *Why three concurrent pairwise calls?* No justification given. State the variance-reduction or quorum argument.
- *Per-shard residualization weight.* How is it fit? On what data? How often re-fit relative to the 6-hour pool refresh?

**7. The conclusion restates the abstract.** Cut it or replace it with what's actually new for a closing paragraph: limitations (single judge, English-only?), the cost of pool maintenance, and a forward-looking claim that does not duplicate paragraph 1.

**8. Minor prose tightening.** "Post-hoc analytical step" and "downstream analytical adjustment" are the same phrase twice in one paragraph. "We position LenSlice as the first such serving system" in the conclusion duplicates the intro's "To our knowledge, this is the first end-to-end system."

## Revised draft

---

We present LenSlice, a serving system that performs length-debiased LLM-as-judge evaluation inline at the request layer. Length bias — the tendency of LLM judges to prefer longer completions independent of quality — is a well-documented confounder in automatic evaluation, and is typically corrected post hoc by regressing judge preference on length after collection. LenSlice instead folds length residualization into the judge inference path itself. Relative to JudgeStream (Reyes and Tanaka, 2023), which introduced inline residualization with a single monolithic judge pool, LenSlice's contribution is a sharded, length-bucketed router that maintains separate live reference pools per bucket, yielding both a substantially larger residual-bias reduction and higher sustained throughput on identical hardware.

LenSlice sits as a router in front of a fleet of judge replicas. Each candidate completion is assigned to one of twelve length buckets (50–100, 100–200, …, 1600+ tokens). Each bucket is served by a shard that holds a live pool of length-matched reference completions sampled from recent production traffic and refreshed every six hours. For each incoming request the router draws three independent references from the matched pool, issues three concurrent pairwise judge calls (used to reduce per-pair variance), applies a per-shard residualization weight fit on a rolling 24-hour window of judged pairs, and returns a debiased win-rate. The per-shard weight isolates within-bucket residual bias that survives length matching.

We benchmark on 12,000 completions sampled from the AlpacaEval-2 test distribution, judged by GPT-4-Turbo. We fit length bias as the coefficient β on log token-length delta in a logistic regression of judge preference; LenSlice reduces β from 0.43 (95% CI [0.41, 0.45]) to 0.04 (95% CI [0.02, 0.06]), a 90% reduction. End-to-end overhead is 38 ms p50 and 71 ms p99 above the unmodified judge call. On a 4×A100 deployment, sustained throughput is 1,840 judgments per second, compared with 1,200/sec reported for JudgeStream on the same hardware.

LenSlice shows that production-grade length debiasing need not be an offline step. Limitations: results are reported for a single judge (GPT-4-Turbo) on a single benchmark, and pool freshness depends on production traffic volume; generalization to other judge families and to domains with skewed length distributions remains open.

---

## Unverified items

- I could not confirm the Reyes and Tanaka (2023) "JudgeStream" citation; please verify it exists and that its 1,200 judgments/sec figure is reported on a comparable 4×A100 configuration. If either is off, the throughput comparison in paragraph 3 needs adjustment.
- The 95% CIs I inserted for β are placeholders illustrating the format; substitute your actual intervals.
- The "rolling 24-hour window" for fitting per-shard residualization weights is a reasonable default I inferred from the 6-hour pool refresh cadence — replace with your actual fitting window if different.
